request.question: What do George Orwell and Jane Smiley have in common?

title: George Orwell: A Life in Pictures
Jane Smiley
Tavis Smiley
George Orwell bibliography
Animal Farm
Barnhill, Jura
Inez Holden
George Orwell
Inside the Whale
Homage to Catalonia
sentences: George Orwell: A Life in Pictures is a 2003 BBC Television docudrama telling the life story of the British author George Orwell.
Chris Langham plays the part of Orwell.
No surviving sound recordings or video of the real George Orwell have been found.
Jane Smiley (born September 26, 1949) is an American novelist.
She won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1992 for her novel "A Thousand Acres" (1991).
Tavis Smiley ( ; born September 13, 1964) is an American talk show host and author.
Smiley was born in Gulfport, Mississippi, and grew up in Bunker Hill, Indiana.
After attending Indiana University, he worked during the late 1980s as an aide to Tom Bradley, the mayor of Los Angeles.
Smiley became a radio commentator in 1991 and, starting in 1996, he hosted the talk show "BET Talk" (later renamed "BET Tonight") on Black Entertainment Television (BET).
After Smiley sold an exclusive interview of Sara Jane Olson to ABC News in 2001, BET declined to renew his contract that year.

Smiley then began hosting "The Tavis Smiley Show" on National Public Radio (NPR) (2002–04) and currently hosts "Tavis Smiley" on the Public Broadcasting Service (PBS) on weekdays and "The Tavis Smiley Show" on Public Radio International (PRI).
From 2010 to 2013, Smiley and Cornel West joined forces to host their own radio talk show, "Smiley & West".
They were featured together interviewing musician Bill Withers in the 2009 documentary film "Still Bill".
He is the new host of "Tavis Talks" on BlogTalkRadio's Tavis Smiley Network.

The bibliography of George Orwell includes journalism, essays, novels and non-fiction books written by the British writer Eric Blair (1903–50), either under his own name or, more usually, under his pen name George Orwell.

Orwell was a prolific writer on topics related to contemporary English society and literary criticism, whom the British newsweekly "The Economist" in 2008 declared "perhaps the 20th century's best chronicler of English culture."
His non-fiction cultural and political criticism constitutes the majority of his work, but Orwell also wrote in several genres of fictional literature.
Animal Farm is an allegorical novella by George Orwell, first published in England on 17 August 1945.
According to Orwell, the book reflects events leading up to the Russian Revolution of 1917 and then on into the Stalinist era of the Soviet Union.
Orwell, a democratic socialist, was a critic of Joseph Stalin and hostile to Moscow-directed Stalinism, an attitude that was critically shaped by his experiences during the Spanish Civil War.
The Soviet Union, he believed, had become a brutal dictatorship, built upon a cult of personality and enforced by a reign of terror.

In a letter to Yvonne Davet, Orwell described "Animal Farm" as a satirical tale against Stalin (""un conte satirique contre Staline""), and in his essay "Why I Write" (1946), wrote that "Animal Farm" was the first book in which he tried, with full consciousness of what he was doing, "to fuse political purpose and artistic purpose into one whole".
Barnhill is a farmhouse situated at grid reference [ NR705970] in the north of the island of Jura in the Scottish Hebrides.
It stands on the site of a larger 15th Century settlement called Cnoc an t-Sabhail; the English name Barnhill having been in use since the early twentieth century.

The house was the home of the English essayist and novelist George Orwell, who lived there intermittently from 1946 until his death in 1950, and who completed his novel "Nineteen Eighty-Four" while living there.
Barnhill is a site of interest for many who are familiar with the life and writing of George Orwell.
Beatrice Inez Lisette (Paget) Holden (21 November 1903- 30 May 1974) was a British writer and Bohemian social figure and journalist, also known for her association with George Orwell.

Born at Wellesbourne, Warwickshire to Wilfred Millington Holden (of the family listed in Burke's Landed Gentry 1952 edition as 'Holden of Bromson', a branch of the family of 'Holden of Hawton and Sibdon'; he served as a Lieutenant in the Bihar Light Horse and 15th Hussars) and Beatrice Mary Byng Paget (the daughter of Herbert Byng Paget, of Darley House, Darley Dale, Derbyshire, of the family listed in Burke's Landed Gentry as 'Paget of Loughborough'), with an elder brother, Wilfred Herbert, born in 1902, she was a cousin of the twins Celia Mary and Mamaine, who became Arthur Koestler's second wife.

Her first memory was her father shooting at- and missing- her mother; their relationship was a fraught one, as was Holden's own relationship with her mother, who was considered to be the second best horsewoman in England.
She kept fifteen chargers, but although her extravagant nature allowed her to visit the Ritz when in London, her daughter did not benefit from it.

According to Bluemel (in 'George Orwell and the Radical Eccentrics', which devotes considerable attention to Holden), Holden's status as 'a dropout from the gentry class who worked as both factory hand and intellectual' is a contributing factor in the value of her work both generally speaking and for feminist scholars.
Eric Arthur Blair (25 June 1903 – 21 January 1950), better known by his pen name George Orwell, was an English novelist, essayist, journalist, and critic.
His work is marked by lucid prose, awareness of social injustice, opposition to totalitarianism, and outspoken support of democratic socialism.
"Inside the Whale" is an essay in three parts written by George Orwell in 1940.
It is primarily a review of "Tropic of Cancer" by Henry Miller with Orwell discursing more widely over English literature in the 1920s and 1930s.

The biblical story of Jonah and the whale is used as a metaphor for accepting experience without seeking to change it, Jonah inside the whale being comfortably protected from the problems of the outside world.
It was published, alongside two other pieces by Orwell, 11 March 1940 by Gollancz in Orwell's first collection of essays, "Inside the Whale and Other Essays".
Homage to Catalonia is George Orwell's personal account of his experiences and observations in the Spanish Civil War.
The first edition was published in the United Kingdom in 1938.
The book was not published in the United States until February 1952, when it appeared with an influential preface by Lionel Trilling.
The only translation published in Orwell's lifetime was into Italian, in December 1948.
A French translation by Yvonne Davet—with whom Orwell corresponded, commenting on her translation and providing explanatory notes—in 1938–39, was not published until five years after Orwell's death.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What do George Orwell and Jane Smiley have in common?
hop1_docs: George Orwell: A Life in Pictures | George Orwell: A Life in Pictures is a 2003 BBC Television docudrama telling the life story of the British author George Orwell.  Chris Langham plays the part of Orwell.  No surviving sound recordings or video of the real George Orwell have been found.

Animal Farm | Animal Farm is an allegorical novella by George Orwell, first published in England on 17 August 1945.  According to Orwell, the book reflects events leading up to the Russian Revolution of 1917 and then on into the Stalinist era of the Soviet Union.  Orwell, a democratic socialist, was a critic of Joseph Stalin and hostile to Moscow-directed Stalinism, an attitude that was critically shaped by his experiences during the Spanish Civil War.  The Soviet Union, he believed, had become a brutal dictatorship, built upon a cult of personality and enforced by a reign of terror.  In a letter to Yvonne Davet, Orwell described "Animal Farm" as a satirical tale against Stalin (""un conte satirique contre Staline""), and in his essay "Why I Write" (1946), wrote that "Animal Farm" was the first book in which he tried, with full consciousness of what he was doing, "to fuse political purpose and artistic purpose into one whole".
Jane Smiley | Jane Smiley (born September 26, 1949) is an American novelist.  She won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1992 for her novel "A Thousand Acres" (1991).

Arnold Mikelson | What do the estates of film stars Vincent Price and Glenn Ford have in common?  And what do each of these estates have in common with valuables owned by Laugh-In’s Arte Johnson, Partridge Family Mom Shirley Jones, and Room 222 star Karen Valentine, along with many other Hollywood stars?

A Good Word for the Vicar of Bray | "A Good Word for the Vicar of Bray" is an essay by the English author George Orwell.  In it Orwell encourages the public-spirited action of planting trees, which may well make up for the harm people do in their lives.  The essay was first published in "Tribune" on 26 April 1946.

Orwell Award | The NCTE George Orwell Award for Distinguished Contribution to Honesty and Clarity in Public Language (the Orwell Award for short), "established in 1975 and given by the National Council of Teachers of English Public Language Award Committee, recognizes writers who have made outstanding contributions to the critical analysis of public discourse."
Some Thoughts on the Common Toad | "Some Thoughts on the Common Toad" is an essay published in 1946 by the English author George Orwell.  It is a eulogy in favour of spring.
hop1_titles: George Orwell: A Life in Pictures
A Good Word for the Vicar of Bray
Some Thoughts on the Common Toad
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: George Orwell (author) and Jane Smiley (novelist)

- George Orwell: described as "British author" (passage 1) and "English author" (passages 5 and 7); author of "Animal Farm" — "first published in England on 17 August 1945" (passage 2); wrote essays published in 1946 (passages 5, 7); called "a democratic socialist" (passage 2).
- Jane Smiley: "Jane Smiley (born September 26, 1949) is an American novelist." and "She won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1992 for her novel 'A Thousand Acres' (1991)." (passage 3).
- Ambiguity / missing fact: passages do not explicitly call George Orwell a "novelist" (he is called "author" and shown to have written a novella and essays). Bridge entity: George Orwell — need confirmation of occupation label "novelist" or equivalent.
- Next-hop retrievals (priority order):
  1. "George Orwell" page — lede/infobox and "Occupation" or "Works" sections (confirm whether Orwell is labeled "novelist" and list of novels/novella).
  2. "Jane Smiley" page — lede/infobox ("Occupation") for direct comparison (already shows "novelist").
  3. "George Orwell bibliography" or section "List of works" / "Novels" (to show novel-length works if needed).
  4. Search keywords: "George Orwell novelist" and "George Orwell occupation novelist" to catch any disambiguation or variant descriptions.
response.hop2_query: George Orwell Jane Smiley both novelists occupation evidence
hop2_docs: Jane Smiley | Jane Smiley (born September 26, 1949) is an American novelist.  She won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1992 for her novel "A Thousand Acres" (1991).

Orwell Site (Orwell, Vermont) | The Orwell Site, designated VT-AD-12 in the state archaeological inventory, is a mainly prehistoric archaeological site in Orwell, Vermont.  The site, first investigated in 1933, is significant as one of the state's few documented examples of a heavily stratified site, with layered evidence of occupation extending back at least 2,000 years, and into colonial times.  It includes a prehistoric Native American burial site with at least 46 grave sites.  It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1977.

George Orwell | Eric Arthur Blair (25 June 1903 – 21 January 1950), better known by his pen name George Orwell, was an English novelist, essayist, journalist, and critic.  His work is marked by lucid prose, awareness of social injustice, opposition to totalitarianism, and outspoken support of democratic socialism.

George Orwell: A Life in Pictures | George Orwell: A Life in Pictures is a 2003 BBC Television docudrama telling the life story of the British author George Orwell.  Chris Langham plays the part of Orwell.  No surviving sound recordings or video of the real George Orwell have been found.

Barnhill, Jura | Barnhill is a farmhouse situated at grid reference [ NR705970] in the north of the island of Jura in the Scottish Hebrides.  It stands on the site of a larger 15th Century settlement called Cnoc an t-Sabhail; the English name Barnhill having been in use since the early twentieth century.  The house was the home of the English essayist and novelist George Orwell, who lived there intermittently from 1946 until his death in 1950, and who completed his novel "Nineteen Eighty-Four" while living there.  Barnhill is a site of interest for many who are familiar with the life and writing of George Orwell.

Smiley's People | Smiley's People is a spy novel by John le Carré, published in 1979.  Featuring British master-spy George Smiley, it is the third and final novel of the "Karla Trilogy", following "Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy" and "The Honourable Schoolboy".  George Smiley is called out of retirement to investigate the death of one of his old agents: a former Soviet general, the head of an Estonian émigré organisation based in London.  Smiley learns the general had discovered information that will lead to a final confrontation with Smiley's nemesis, the Soviet spymaster Karla.

George Orwell bibliography | The bibliography of George Orwell includes journalism, essays, novels and non-fiction books written by the British writer Eric Blair (1903–50), either under his own name or, more usually, under his pen name George Orwell.  Orwell was a prolific writer on topics related to contemporary English society and literary criticism, whom the British newsweekly "The Economist" in 2008 declared "perhaps the 20th century's best chronicler of English culture."  His non-fiction cultural and political criticism constitutes the majority of his work, but Orwell also wrote in several genres of fictional literature.
hop2_titles: George Orwell: A Life in Pictures
response.summary_2: {"summary":"Both George Orwell and Jane Smiley are novelists. George Orwell was an English novelist and essayist. Jane Smiley is an American novelist who won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1992."}
response.answer: They are both novelists (George Orwell was an English novelist and essayist; Jane Smiley is an American novelist who won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction).
